1: But now, thus says YHWH who created you, O Jacob, and he who formed you, O Israel: Do not fear, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by name, you are mine. The three grounds of the do-not-fear: creation (I made you), redemption (I bought you back), and name-calling (I know you personally). The called-you-by-name is the ultimate statement of covenant intimacy — not the mass-address to a category but the individual naming that constitutes personal relationship. In the NT, the Good Shepherd calls his own sheep by name and leads them out (John 10:3); the risen Jesus calls Mary Magdalene by name and she recognizes him (John 20:16). The name-called identity of the covenant community is fulfilled in the Shepherd who knows each sheep by name.
2: When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and through the rivers, they will not sweep over you. When you walk through the fire, you will not be scorched; the flame will not burn you. The four elements of crisis (waters, rivers, fire, flame) are the covenant-crossing conditions: YHWH does not promise the absence of deep water and fire but the presence that prevents drowning and burning. This is not protection from trial but protection in trial. In the NT, Christian baptism is the passing through water with Christ (Romans 6:3-4: baptized into his death); the fiery trial that tests faith (1 Peter 4:12-13) is the fire through which the covenant community passes without being destroyed. The NT adds: if we suffer with him we will also be glorified with him (Romans 8:17).
3: For I am YHWH your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior; I give Egypt as your ransom, Cush and Seba in your place. YHWH as Savior — the title that the NT will apply to Jesus: a Savior has been born to you (Luke 2:11); Jesus Christ our Savior (Titus 1:4, Isaiah 2:13, Isaiah 3:6). The ransom-price language anticipates the Servant's ransom: the Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many (Matthew 20:28, Mark 10:45), where the ransom is not nations but the Servant's own nefesh (Isaiah 53:10-12). Egypt/Cush/Seba as the ransom-price in historical terms is the theological type: YHWH pays the cost of Israel's redemption; at the cross, the cost is paid in the Son's own life.
4: Because you are precious in my sight and honored, and I love you, I will give people in return for you, nations instead of your life. The declaration of love for the covenant people — precious, honored, loved — is the theological ground of the ransom-price. YHWH gives nations for Israel because Israel is loved. In the NT: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son (John 3:16) — the love precedes and grounds the giving; God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). The precious-in-my-sight-and-loved is the NT's beloved (Ephesians 1:6: accepted in the Beloved).
5: Do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bring your children from the east and gather you from the west. The second do-not-fear oracle (cf. v 1) grounds fear-removal in divine presence: I am with you. The Immanuel promise of 7:14 is the eschatological form of the covenant-presence name. In the NT: I am with you always, to the very end of the age (Matthew 28:20) — the risen Jesus guarantees his presence as the grounds of the mission. The gathering from east and west is the Gentile ingathering of the eschatological age: many will come from east and west and take their places at the feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Matthew 8:11).
6: I will say to the north, Give them up! and to the south, Do not hold them back. Bring my sons from afar and my daughters from the ends of the earth. The cosmic command to the four compass points to release YHWH's scattered people is the eschatological ingathering oracle. In the NT: the Son of Man will send his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other (Matthew 24:31). The gathering from the ends of the earth is the global scope of the gospel mission — the diaspora of exile reversed by the diaspora of Pentecost (Acts 2:5-11: devout Jews from every nation under heaven).
7: Everyone who is called by my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I formed and made. The called-by-my-name is the covenant-identity marker — the name-called community (cf. v 1). Created for my glory extends the purpose of creation: humanity is made not for its own self-expression but for YHWH's glory-display. In the NT: you were bought with a price; therefore glorify God in your body (1 Corinthians 6:20); we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works (Ephesians 2:10). The new covenant community is created in Christ for the same glory-purpose that YHWH declares for Israel here — the creation-for-glory is now the new-creation-for-glory.
8: Bring out the people who are blind, though they have eyes, and the deaf, though they have ears. The blind-and-deaf people — Israel — are summoned as witnesses to the cosmic court case. The people who have eyes but do not see and have ears but do not hear (Jeremiah 5:21, Ezekiel 12:2) are YHWH's witnesses despite their spiritual impairment. In the NT, this paradox is the mission situation: Jesus heals the physically blind as the sign of healing the spiritually blind; yet the Pharisees who can see physically remain blind spiritually (John 9:39-41). The blind-witness is the community that testifies to YHWH despite its own failures to perceive — the same community Jesus commissions: you will be my witnesses (Acts 1:8) before they receive the Spirit that opens eyes.
9: Let all the nations assemble and the peoples gather. Which of them foretold this and proclaimed to us the former things? Let them bring their witnesses to prove they are right, so people may hear and say, It is true. The nations summoned to the court cannot produce witnesses because no other god predicted history. The proof-from-prophecy is the epistemological argument for YHWH's uniqueness: only YHWH knows the future and announces it in advance. In the NT, the fulfillment of Messianic prophecy is the same argument: these things happened so that the scripture would be fulfilled (John 19:28, 36) — the evidence-from-fulfilled-prediction is YHWH's courtroom proof applied to the identity of Jesus.
10: You are my witnesses, declares YHWH, and my servant whom I have chosen — so that you may know and believe me and understand that I am he. Before me no god was formed, nor will there be after me. The witnesses and servant — Israel as a whole and the individual Servant — are the dual testimony to YHWH's unique identity. The you-are-my-witnesses is quoted by Jesus in Acts 1:8 (you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem... to the ends of the earth) as the commission given to the apostles. The witness-bearing community is the NT church; the servant is the Messiah who enables the witness by his own testimony of life, death, and resurrection. I am he — the Hebrew anî hû is the same Johannine ego eimi that Jesus uses (John 8:58, Isaiah 13:19): I am he is the divine self-identification that places Jesus within YHWH's unique identity.
11: I, even I, am YHWH, and apart from me there is no savior. The emphatic double I (anî anî YHWH) — YHWH identifying himself as the sole savior — is the monotheistic declaration that the NT applies to Jesus. There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among people by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). The beside-me-no-savior of YHWH becomes the no-other-name of Jesus — the NT placing Jesus in the category that Isaiah 43:11 reserves for YHWH alone. This is the Christological logic of the NT's high Christology: if YHWH alone saves and Jesus saves, then Jesus shares in YHWH's identity.
12: I have revealed, and saved, and proclaimed, and there was no strange god among you; you are my witnesses, declares YHWH, and I am God. The three-fold action — revealed, saved, proclaimed — is the divine curriculum of covenant history. No foreign god participated in these saving acts: the Exodus was YHWH alone, no Baal or Marduk or Nisroch (cf. Isaiah 36:18-20) assisted. You are my witnesses to this history of sole-savior action. In the NT: the resurrection is YHWH's final revealed-saved-proclaimed act in history — the gospel is the proclamation of the saving revelation that the witnesses (Acts 1:21-22) are commissioned to declare.
13: Also from eternity I am he, and there is none who can deliver from my hand; when I act, who can reverse it? The from-eternity I am he extends YHWH's sole-savior status backward to eternity: he has always been the only God. The none-who-can-deliver-from-my-hand is the sovereignty claim: no external power can undo what YHWH establishes. In the NT, Jesus uses the same hand-language: I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand (John 10:28-29). The hand of YHWH from which no one is snatched is the hand of the Good Shepherd — YHWH's eternal sovereignty expressed in the Shepherd's protecting grasp.
14: Thus says YHWH, your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: For your sake I will send to Babylon and make them all flee as fugitives — even the Chaldeans, in the ships in which they take pride. YHWH sends his agent (Cyrus, historically) to Babylon for the sake of Israel — the covenant motive (for your sake) driving the geopolitical act. The pride of the Chaldeans (Babylon's ships on the Euphrates, symbols of imperial power) becomes the symbol of their humiliation. In the NT, the fall of Babylon (Revelation 18:2: Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the great!) echoes this oracle — the maritime traders weep (Revelation 18:17-19) as the ships that carried their wealth become useless.
15: I am YHWH, your Holy One, the Creator of Israel, your King. The four-title summary: Holy One, Creator, Israel's-God, and King. The King title points to the Davidic Messianic expectation that runs through Isaiah: the child of Isaiah 9:6-7 is the Davidic king; the servant/Branch of Isaiah 11:1-5 is the royal deliverer. In the NT: Jesus is the King of Israel (John 1:49, Isaiah 12:13) and the King of kings (Revelation 19:16) — the one who fulfills what YHWH claims here as his own royal identity.
16: Thus says YHWH, who makes a way in the sea and a path in the mighty waters. The new-Exodus introduction: YHWH identified by his past act (the parting of the Red Sea) is about to do something analogous but greater. The way-in-the-sea language is picked up in John the Baptist's role: Mark 1:2-3 cites both Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3 (prepare the way of the Lord, make straight paths for him) to describe John as the one making the eschatological way. The way-in-the-sea becomes the way in the wilderness (v 19), and both are fulfilled in the one who is himself the Way (John 14:6).
17: Who leads out chariot and horse, army and warrior; they lie down together, they will not rise up — they are extinguished, quenched like a wick. The Exodus destruction of Pharaoh's army (Exodus 14:28) is the past event from which the new Exodus is predicted: the first enemy destroyed in the sea provides the template for the eschatological enemy's destruction. They will not rise up; they are extinguished. In the NT, the final enemy whose destruction mirrors this is death itself: the last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Corinthians 15:26). The Exodus destruction of the Egyptian army is the type of the resurrection's destruction of death — the ultimate extinguishing of what opposed YHWH's people.
18: Do not remember the former things or consider the things of old. The command to forget the former Exodus is startling — YHWH's greatest redemptive act is being overshadowed by a coming act. The former-thing forgetting is not historical amnesia but the recognition that the new act will be so much greater that the old becomes the lesser type. In the NT: Paul counts his former things as loss for the sake of gaining Christ (Philippians 3:7-8) and forgets what is behind while straining toward what is ahead (Philippians 3:13). The do-not-remember-the-former-things is the eschatological posture of the one who lives in the new creation.
19: See, I am doing a new thing; now it springs up — do you not perceive it? I am making a way in the wilderness and streams in the wasteland. The new thing (hadashah — the eschatological novelty) is the second great NT theme verse: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away, the new has come (2 Corinthians 5:17). The way-in-the-wilderness anticipates John the Baptist making the way straight (Isaiah 40:3, cited in all four Gospels) and Jesus declaring himself the Way. The streams-in-the-wasteland is the living water Jesus offers (John 4:10, Isaiah 7:37-38). This verse is the seed from which the entire NT theology of newness — new covenant, new creation, new heart — grows.
20: The wild animals will honor me, the jackals and the ostriches — because I provide water in the wilderness, rivers in the wasteland, to give drink to my chosen people. The creation-response to YHWH's provision extends even to the wilderness creatures: the jackals and ostriches (symbols of desolation in Isaiah's judgment oracles, Isaiah 13:21-22, Isaiah 34:13) now honor YHWH because the wilderness they inhabit has been watered by his provision. In the NT, the restoration of creation is the scope of the gospel: the creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed (Romans 8:19). The wild animals honoring YHWH anticipate the new creation's harmony — the wolf lying with the lamb (Isaiah 65:25).
21: The people I formed for myself will declare my praise. The formed-for-myself people are the chosen witnesses of vv 10-12, now formed specifically for doxology: their purpose is praise. In the NT: you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light (1 Peter 2:9). The praise-declaring purpose of the covenant community is the NT form of YHWH's word here — the community exists to proclaim what YHWH has done.
22: Yet you have not called on me, Jacob; you have grown weary of me, Israel. The indictment: the people formed for praise (v 21) have not prayed. The weariness of Israel toward YHWH inverts the weariness the Servant never showed (Isaiah 42:4: he will not grow faint or be discouraged). In the NT, the contrast is between Israel's failure to call and the NT community's commission to pray without ceasing (1 Thessalonians 5:17); between growing weary of YHWH and running with endurance the race set before us, fixing our eyes on Jesus (Hebrews 12:1-2). The community that forgets to call on its Creator is the shadow that the Servant's perseverance corrects.
23: You have not brought me sheep for burnt offerings or honored me with your sacrifices. I have not burdened you with grain offerings or wearied you with frankincense. The accusation is that Israel's neglect of YHWH is not YHWH's fault — he made worship accessible and undemanding, but they still did not come. In the NT, Jesus makes the same point differently: come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest — my yoke is easy and my burden is light (Matthew 11:28-30). The true sacrifice that YHWH desires (a broken spirit, a broken and contrite heart — Psalms 51:17) is not burdensome; the failure to worship is the community's willful neglect, not YHWH's inaccessibility.
24: You have not bought any fragrant calamus for me or lavished the fat of your sacrifices on me. Instead, you have burdened me with your sins and wearied me with your offenses. The reversal: Israel accuses YHWH of making worship burdensome (the common complaint against religious obligation) but the indictment flips it — they have burdened YHWH with their sins. The weight of sin borne by YHWH is the image that becomes explicit in Isaiah 53:4-6: surely he bore our infirmities... YHWH laid on him the iniquity of us all. The sin-burden that Israel placed on YHWH is borne by the Servant as the culmination of what YHWH bore throughout the covenant's history.
25: I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins. The emphatic double I (anî hû anî) — the same structure as v 11 and v 13 — now applied to forgiveness: YHWH is the sole forgiver as he is the sole savior. The for-my-own-sake grounds forgiveness in YHWH's character, not in Israel's merit. The blotting-out (mocheh) is the image of wiping a slate clean — the same image in Colossians 2:14 (having blotted out the handwriting of ordinances that was against us). The I-will-not-remember-your-sins is the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:34 (I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more). The scribes at Capernaum ask the right question: who can forgive sins but God alone? (Mark 2:7) — and Jesus answers by forgiving, claiming the authority reserved for the anî hû of Isaiah 43:25.
26: Remind me; let us argue the case together; state your case, that you may be proved right. YHWH invites Israel to the court: if you have a counter-argument, make it. The legal-argument invitation exposes the emptiness of any self-justification attempt — not because the process is rigged but because Israel has no case. In the NT: if we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves and the truth is not in us (1 John 1:8). The court that YHWH opens in Isaiah 43:26 is the court in which the entire human race stands — the day of judgment at which no one can stand on their own righteousness. Only the righteousness credited through faith (Romans 3:21-22) provides ground to stand in that court.
27: Your first father sinned, and your representatives have rebelled against me. The first father — either Adam (whose sin in the garden established the pattern) or Jacob (whose history of striving-with-God prefigures Israel's) — sinned from the beginning. The mediators (representatives — the priests and prophets) amplified rather than corrected the sin. In the NT, Paul traces the same genealogy of sin: sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death spread to all people (Romans 5:12). The first father's sin is the original problem for which the Servant is the solution: just as through one man's sin, so through one man's righteous act, life comes to all (Romans 5:18-19).
28: So I will bring disgrace on the dignitaries of your sanctuary, and I will devote Jacob to total destruction and Israel to scorn. The judgment that the entire chapter moves toward: the sanctuary's dignitaries (the priesthood that failed to mediate) are disgraced; the covenant community is given to destruction (cherem — the holy war ban). The destruction of the sanctuary and its leadership is the 70 CE event that Jesus predicted (Matthew 24:2: not one stone will be left on another). But the destruction of the old system opens the way for the new: the veil torn from top to bottom (Matthew 27:51) when the Servant dies is the disgracing of the old sanctuary's boundary that makes YHWH's presence accessible to all.
